topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on my desk like a ticking time bomb. It was thick, cream-colored, and addressed in elegant, looping script—a script I hadn't seen in fifteen years. My hands, usually steady enough to grade papers under pressure, were trembling slightly as I slid a thumbnail under the seal. Every shadow in the dimly lit classroom seemed to shift, elongating into something watchful. Who would send this? It felt like an intrusion, a deliberate breach of the carefully constructed quiet of my routine. I unfolded the paper slowly, the rustle of the thick stock sounding deafening in the silence. Inside, nestled against tissue paper, was a small, perfectly polished river stone, cool against my palm, and a handwritten note: "Thinking of you, Ms. Eleanor." My breath hitched. I glanced around the empty hallway. Had someone been watching me while I was distracted? The stone felt heavy, charged with an unknown significance.

[story 2]

I kept checking the lock on the supply cabinet, even though I knew it was secure. The mail slot rattled with a sound that wasn't the usual delivery truck; it was too deliberate, too close. My eyes darted to the window, convinced a figure was pressed against the glass, observing the contents of my study. When I finally retrieved the package, my heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat demanding attention. It was a vintage fountain pen, a gift from Marcus, a student who had left town abruptly after a difficult semester. The gift itself was innocuous, yet the way it had appeared—silent, unannounced, right in the middle of my locked office—made the hairs on my arms stand straight up. I gripped the pen so tightly my knuckles turned white. I started counting the seconds, listening intently for any sound that suggested the package hadn't been alone when I received it.

[story 3]

The afternoon light slanted through the blinds, casting stripes across the floor, making the dust motes dance like tiny, malicious spirits. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting the frame to suddenly warp or someone to materialize in the doorway. When I finally picked up the small, intricately carved wooden bird—a keepsake from old Thomas—I felt a cold prickle crawl up my spine. It was too specific. Too personal. I examined the wrapping paper, running my thumb over the embossed lettering, searching for any imperfection, any sign that it wasn't what it seemed. Every small movement felt amplified, scrutinized by an unseen audience. I placed the bird on the corner of my desk, then immediately reached for the heavy ledger, needing the tangible weight of my work to anchor me against the unsettling feeling that the entire building was suddenly too crowded.
